did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier to detect and classify skin cancer (normal vs. abnormal) from Terahertz images. Specifically, it employed Gabor filter feature extraction followed by a two-layer feed-forward neural network trained with the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm to achieve a classification accuracy of 94.117%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a skin cancer detection system using Terahertz images, Gabor filter feature extraction, and an Artificial Neural Network (ANN). It includes a specific methodology, dataset collection, simulation results (94.117% accuracy), and performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection based on Terahertz Images by using Gabor filter and Artificial Neural network
journal: IOP Conference Series: Materials Science and Engineering
brief_summary: This study proposes a non-invasive skin cancer detection system utilizing Terahertz pulse imaging (TPI) to distinguish between normal and abnormal skin tissues. The methodology involves preprocessing THz images via resizing and median filtering, followed by feature extraction using Gabor filters with five scales and eight orientations. These features are classified using a two-layer feed-forward Artificial Neural Network (ANN) trained with the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm. The system was evaluated on a dataset of THz images, achieving a total classification accuracy of 94.117%, with a sensitivity of 71.42% and specificity of 100% on the test set. The results indicate that combining Gabor feature extraction with ANN provides an efficient and accurate method for early skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies images into 'normal' and 'abnormal' (skin cancer) categories. The dataset contains THz images of skin cancer (abnormal tissue) and normal tissue. Specific histological subtypes (e.g., melanoma, BCC) are not reported; the class is broadly defined as 'skin cancer' or 'abnormal tissue' characterized by increased blood content.
Used as the negative class for binary classification against skin cancer/abnormal tissue. The dataset includes THz images of normal skin tissue.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin tissue into 'normal' and 'abnormal' (skin cancer) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a two-layer feed-forward Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with a softmax output layer to classify Terahertz images. The target classes are binary: 'normal' and 'abnormal' (skin cancer). The model outputs class probabilities/vectors (e.g., cancer tissue output = [1 0 0 0], normal output = [0 1 0 0]) which are compared against a threshold (0.7) to determine the final diagnostic label. Performance is evaluated using accuracy (94.117%), sensitivity (71.42%), and specificity (100%).

datasets_used.name: Custom Terahertz Pulse Imaging (TPI) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of Terahertz pulse imaging (TPI) images of skin tissue, collected by the authors. The dataset includes images of normal skin tissue and abnormal skin tissue (skin cancer). The images were captured using TPI technique inside and outside the body with different depths. Specific source (hospital/lab) is not named, but the context implies a local collection for the study.

use.training: Used to train the two-layer feed-forward Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm. The input feature vectors were divided into training and testing sets, but specific counts for the training set are not reported.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the network performance. Specifically, 7 abnormal images and 7 normal images were used for explicit testing in Tables 1 and 2. Additionally, a total of 14 test images (7 abnormal, 7 normal) were used to calculate sensitivity and specificity, and a total of 34 images (implied by the accuracy calculation denominator 7+7+20) were used to determine the total classification accuracy of 94.117%.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize performance and calculate sensitivity, specificity, and total accuracy by comparing predicted classes against actual classes.
To quantify the classifier's ability to correctly identify abnormal (cancer) and normal tissues, respectively, based on true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.
To evaluate the performance of the neural network during the training phase.
To determine the final diagnostic label (normal vs. abnormal) by comparing the network output vector values against a fixed threshold of 0.7.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy (94.117%), sensitivity (71.42%), and specificity (100%) based on a confusion matrix and threshold-based decision rules.

Custom Terahertz Pulse Imaging (TPI) Dat.notes: The dataset consisted of THz images of normal and abnormal (skin cancer) tissue. The test set for sensitivity/specificity calculation included 7 abnormal images and 7 normal images. The total accuracy calculation included 34 images (7 abnormal + 7 normal + 20 additional images). On the 7 abnormal test images, 5 were correctly detected and 2 failed. On the 7 normal test images, all 7 were correctly detected. The network was trained using the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm with a hidden layer of 50 neurons.

architecture_used.input_size: Input feature vector derived from Gabor filter extraction on resized [150x150] images; specific input dimension count not explicitly stated but derived from Gabor parameters

architecture_details.layers: Two layers (one hidden layer, one output layer)
architecture_details.feature_extractor: Gabor filter bank (5 scales, 8 orientations, kernel size 39x39, down-sampling factors 4x4)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to [150x150] pixels using the imresize function
Median filtering with a [3x3] kernel to eliminate noise while preserving edges
Normalization of Gabor feature vectors to zero mean and unit variance
conclusions: The proposed system using Gabor feature extraction and an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier is efficient and capable of detecting skin cancer tissue with high classification accuracy (94.117%).
The method provides a rapid and non-invasive diagnosis, with simulation results indicating less consumption time for cancer detection compared to other methods.
Gabor feature extraction effectively focuses on very small and hidden patterns under the skin, which simplifies the classifier's work for patient and health pattern analysis.
The system achieved a sensitivity of 71.42% (5/7 abnormal images correctly detected) and a specificity of 100% (7/7 normal images correctly detected) on the test set.

Terahertz pulse imaging (TPI) combined with pattern recognition tools offers a potential rapid and non-invasive method to diagnose diseases, leveraging the sensitivity of T-ray imaging to blood content changes in cancer tissue.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small, custom dataset with no reported total image count or standard train/validation/test splits; only 7 abnormal and 7 normal images were explicitly used for sensitivity/specificity testing.
The dataset lacks histological subtyping (e.g., melanoma vs. basal cell carcinoma), classifying all 'abnormal' tissue broadly as skin cancer, which limits clinical specificity.
There is no external validation; the system was evaluated solely on a local, non-public dataset collected by the authors, raising concerns about generalizability.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) or confidence intervals for the reported accuracy (94.117%), sensitivity (71.42%), or specificity (100%).

The classification threshold of 0.7 was applied to a 4-neuron softmax output (where normal/cancer targets were [0 1 0 0] and [1 0 0 0] respectively), a non-standard implementation that may introduce ambiguity in class assignment logic.
The model achieved 100% specificity but only 71.42% sensitivity, indicating a high rate of false negatives (missed cancers) in the test set (2 out of 7 abnormal cases failed detection).

The hardware and software environment (Intel i5-2520M, 4GB RAM, MATLAB 2016a) is outdated, and computational efficiency claims are not benchmarked against modern standards or other state-of-the-art methods.
The paper does not address potential data leakage or overfitting risks given the extremely small sample size and the use of a simple 2-layer ANN with only 4 training epochs.
